GGUF (llama.cpp format): optimized for CPU inference and edge deployment; quantization built-in; portable across hardware; use for local/edge deployments
ONNX: open standard for cross-framework model exchange; useful when deploying PyTorch models to non-PyTorch runtimes (TensorRT, ONNX Runtime); good for classical ML models

TensorRT format: NVIDIA-specific compilation for maximum GPU inference performance; non-portable (tied to GPU architecture and CUDA version); use for latency-critical GPU deployments where you control the hardware
idealAnswerFollowups: What security review would you do before loading a model checkpoint from an external source?
How do you handle the case where your model architecture changes but you need to load old checkpoint weights?

question: How do you set up alerting and on-call for an ML system in production?
idealAnswerCore: ML system alerting requires layered alerts covering technical health (latency, error rate, availability), model quality (prediction distribution drift, business metric degradation), and data pipeline health — with clear ownership, on-call rotation, and severity-based escalation.
idealAnswerFraming: Effective alerting is about signal-to-noise ratio: alert on things that require action, not just things that are interesting. Over-alerting causes alert fatigue; under-alerting causes prolonged incidents.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Technical alerts (immediate action): p99 latency > SLO, error rate > threshold, serving pod restarts, GPU memory > 90%, model endpoint down — page on-call immediately

Model quality alerts (investigate): prediction distribution shifts significantly from baseline, business metric drops >X% day-over-day, feature drift detected in data pipeline — alert during business hours
Data pipeline alerts: upstream data source missing expected records, feature values out of expected range, pipeline job failures — alert data team, not model team

Alert design principles: every alert should have a corresponding runbook; alerts should be actionable (if you can't act on it, don't alert); tune thresholds to minimize false positives while catching real issues

On-call rotation: define clear handoff procedures (state of current incidents, known issues, upcoming deployments); review alert volume weekly and eliminate noisy alerts; conduct post-mortems for every severity-1 incident
idealAnswerFollowups: How do you distinguish between a model quality issue and a data quality issue when an alert fires?
What's the right severity for an alert that indicates model predictions are 5% less accurate than last week?

question: What is the purpose of an evaluation set (holdout set) and what are common mistakes in constructing one?
idealAnswerCore: An evaluation set is a representative sample of real-world data held back from training to provide an unbiased estimate of model performance. Common mistakes include test set leakage, lack of temporal correctness, and lack of diversity that causes overoptimistic offline metrics.
idealAnswerFraming: The quality of your evaluation set determines the reliability of your offline metrics as predictors of production performance. A bad eval set is worse than no eval set because it gives false confidence.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Test set contamination (leakage): training data that 'leaks' into the eval set inflates metrics — occurs through random splits that ignore temporal ordering, data augmentation applied before splitting, or preprocessing fit on full dataset
Temporal split for time-series data: always split chronologically (train on past, test on future) — random splits let the model 'cheat' by seeing future patterns during training

Distribution mismatch: eval set must represent production traffic distribution including edge cases, rare classes, and domain variation — if you only test on easy examples, you won't catch production failures
Annotation quality: if human labels have disagreements, eval set accuracy is limited by inter-annotator agreement — measure and report label quality, not just model performance
Dynamic eval sets: maintain a 'challenge set' of known hard examples that grow over time as production failures are collected; combine with random eval set for comprehensive assessment
idealAnswerFollowups: How do you construct a representative evaluation set when your production traffic distribution is unknown at the time of development?
What's the minimum size for a reliable evaluation set, and how do you compute it?

question: How do you handle model serving when the model is too large to fit in GPU memory at full precision?
idealAnswerCore: When a model exceeds single-GPU memory, you have three main strategies: quantization (reduce precision to fit more in VRAM), model parallelism (split across multiple GPUs), or CPU offloading (use RAM + swap at the cost of latency).
idealAnswerFraming: GPU memory is the primary constraint in LLM deployment. Show you understand the memory math and the quality-efficiency tradeoffs of each approach.
idealAnswerKeyPoints: Quick memory math: a model with N billion parameters requires approximately 2N GB in FP16, N GB in INT8, or 0.5N GB in INT4 — a 70B model needs ~140GB FP16, ~70GB INT8, ~35GB INT4
Quantization options: bitsandbytes INT8 (minimal quality loss, ~50% memory reduction), GPTQ/AWQ INT4 (significant memory reduction, small quality loss), GGUF Q4 (CPU inference)
Multi-GPU tensor parallelism: split attention heads and FFN layers across GPUs; requires NVLink or fast interconnect for acceptable performance; vLLM and TensorRT-LLM support this natively
CPU offloading with Accelerate: offload some layers to CPU RAM; allows running models that don't fit in GPU VRAM but with significant latency penalty (only acceptable for batch inference)
Practical recommendation: INT8 quantization for production serving when quality matters; INT4 (GPTQ or AWQ) when memory is severely constrained and quality loss is acceptable on your task
idealAnswerFollowups: How would you measure the quality impact of quantization for your specific use case?
What's the latency difference between INT8 quantization on a single GPU vs. FP16 on two GPUs with tensor parallelism?
